prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
“Deep-work marathons.”

The fetish for booking half-day blocks of interruption-free silence has become the productivity equivalent of kale: obviously good for you, so good that we stopped asking whether we actually like it—or need it.

What’s overrated is not deep work itself (distraction-free concentration is real magic) but the prescription that the only way to produce “real” knowledge-work is to disappear into a cave for four-hour stretches. Most people’s jobs are stitched together from 15- to 45-minute cycles of reading, writing, chatting, waiting, and revising. When those natural pulses are forced into a rigid “one block to rule them all” schedule, three things happen:

1. The cost of a single interruption explodes. One stray Slack message can blow up an entire morning, so instead of becoming resilient to interruptions we become psychologically brittle.

2. The work that doesn’t fit inside the marble temple of Deep Work—quick decisions, peer mentoring, polishing the ugly 80 % draft—gets stigmatized as “shallow” and is pushed to the edges of the day when energy is gone.

3. People who can’t command long, unbroken hours (parents, managers, resident physicians, anyone in a client-facing role) conclude they’re broken, adopt heroic wake-up-at-4-a.m. routines, and burn out.

Focused concentration is a cognitive super-power; turning it into a binary lifestyle brand is not. The underrated move is to get better at *rapid* depth—dropping into focus for 20 minutes, surfacing, then dropping again—so the craft integrates with, rather than escapes from, the messy collaborative reality of modern knowledge work.
